replaceability.primary_mechanism: Building Things 2035 moderate (66): Humanoid robots (~$10K) with improved dexterity operate across most structured construction tasks including electrical rough-in, plumbing assembly, and interior finishing in new-build standardized formats. Autonomous heavy equipment handles earthmoving and grading with minimal supervision. AI systems manage full project lifecycle from design through punch-list. Remaining gap: historic preservation and bespoke architectural work, emergency structural repairs, work in confined or hazardous spaces requiring improvisation, and complex multi-system troubleshooting in occupied buildings.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Buying & Selling 2035 moderate (88): Score plateau reflects near-saturation of AI capability across wholesale, retail, and e-commerce functions. AI handles the full sales cycle autonomously for most product categories. Humanoid store associates (~$10K) manage physical retail operations. Remaining gap: the same high-touch selling functions — enterprise relationship management, luxury consultation, and novel product categories requiring human taste-making and cultural context that resist automation even as AI capabilities mature.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Care & Health 2035 moderate (82): Humanoid care robots (~$10K) perform most structured physical care tasks in institutional settings — assisted living, rehabilitation exercises, vital sign monitoring with physical intervention capability. AI systems manage chronic disease treatment plans autonomously with physician oversight. Remaining gap: complex wound care requiring tactile judgment, neonatal and pediatric physical care, psychiatric crisis intervention, community health work requiring cultural navigation, and the irreducible human elements of compassionate end-of-life care.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Feeding & Hosting 2035 moderate (73): Humanoid robots (~$10K) handle most repetitive kitchen tasks (prep, dishwashing, standardized cooking) and hotel housekeeping at commercial scale. AI sommeliers and menu designers operate at expert level. Ghost kitchens are majority robot-operated. Remaining gap: creative culinary work, front-of-house hospitality at fine-dining level, event management requiring real-time human coordination, culturally specific food preparation requiring tacit knowledge, and the emotional labor of hospitality in boutique and luxury settings.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Governing & Protecting 2035 moderate (68): AI handles the majority of bureaucratic governance functions autonomously — tax processing, benefits administration, licensing, code enforcement. Humanoid robots (~$10K) assist in military logistics, border security, and structured patrol roles. AI legal systems draft and review contracts and regulations at expert level. Remaining gap: elected representation and democratic accountability, complex judicial reasoning in novel cases, intelligence work requiring human networks, hostage negotiation, and the fundamental requirement for human authority in use-of-force decisions.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Land & Sea 2035 moderate (66): Agricultural robots (~$10K) with improved dexterity commercially viable for outdoor fruit and vegetable harvesting across most crop types. Autonomous livestock management systems handle feeding, health monitoring, and basic herding. AI-managed vertical farms scale to significant food production. Remaining gap: complex mixed farming requiring seasonal adaptation, artisanal and subsistence agriculture in the Global South, aquaculture in open-water environments, forestry requiring judgment about ecosystem health, and the transition costs that keep much of global agriculture labor-intensive.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Learning & Teaching 2035 moderate (55): AI handles the majority of knowledge delivery, assessment, and instructional planning functions. Humanoid tutoring robots (~$10K) provide embodied presence in some structured learning environments. AI systems manage individualized learning pathways at scale. Remaining gap: the persistent human preference for human teachers, especially for younger children; social-emotional learning; creative mentorship; physical education and hands-on lab supervision; classroom community building; and the pastoral care dimensions of education that families and societies assign to human educators.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity
replaceability.notes: Revised trajectory (baseline 18→28). Was 54.

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Maintaining & Fixing 2035 moderate (63): Humanoid robots (~$10K) handle routine maintenance tasks across standardized commercial and residential settings — changing fixtures, basic plumbing repairs, appliance servicing with AI-guided diagnostics. Autonomous utility infrastructure management covers most routine operations. Remaining gap: complex diagnostic repair requiring experience-based intuition, emergency infrastructure repair, work in hazardous or confined spaces, non-standard building systems requiring adaptive reasoning, and the customer-facing aspects of home services.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Making Meaning 2035 moderate (90): AI creative systems produce work indistinguishable from human output across virtually all media formats. AI handles curation, programming, and cultural production at institutional scale. Remaining gap: live embodied performance (theater, concert, sport), authentic cultural voice representing community experience, creative leadership requiring taste and cultural moment-reading, and the philosophical questions about AI-generated meaning that sustain demand for human creative expression in fine arts.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Making Things 2035 moderate (97): Near-complete automation of manufacturing across all standard product categories globally. Humanoid robots (~$10K) make automation cost-effective even in previously low-wage manufacturing regions. Remaining gap: truly novel product prototyping, artisanal craft production, and edge cases requiring human-level adaptive reasoning in complex assembly — a narrow and shrinking residual.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Money & Data 2035 moderate (96): Near-complete AI capability across financial services, software development, and data management. AI handles full-stack development, quantitative research, and financial advisory at expert level. Remaining gap: frontier financial innovation, the most complex systems architecture decisions, regulatory negotiations requiring political judgment, and fiduciary decisions where legal accountability demands human principals.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Moving Things 2035 moderate (80): Autonomous vehicles operate across most road conditions. Humanoid robots (~$10K) handle most loading, unloading, and last-mile delivery tasks. Autonomous ships operate on major commercial routes. Drone delivery is standard for light parcels in urban areas. Remaining gap: extreme weather driving, complex urban delivery requiring building access negotiation, hazardous materials transport requiring human judgment, search and rescue logistics, and the most complex multimodal supply chain disruptions.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Thinking & Leading 2035 moderate (94): AI matches human expert performance across virtually all professional and administrative service domains. AI manages complex multi-stakeholder projects autonomously. Remaining gap: visionary leadership, complex organizational transformation, novel legal arguments in unprecedented cases, breakthrough scientific thinking, and the shrinking but persistent premium on human judgment in high-stakes professional decisions.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Building Things 2040 moderate (72): Advanced humanoid robots with near-human dexterity handle the majority of physical construction tasks across both new-build and renovation projects. Fully autonomous construction sites operate for standardized building types. AI architects generate and iterate buildable designs end-to-end. Remaining gap: highly creative architectural realization, disaster-response structural work, complex adaptive reuse of heritage structures, and novel engineering solutions for unprecedented building challenges.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Buying & Selling 2040 moderate (90): Marginal gains from improved humanoid dexterity in physical retail and better AI handling of edge cases in dispute resolution and complex returns. Near-complete automation of wholesale distribution chains. Remaining gap: a persistent ~10% of selling tasks requiring genuine human connection — high-stakes negotiations, culturally sensitive market entry, luxury brand ambassadorship, and novel product evangelism where human authenticity drives purchasing decisions.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Care & Health 2040 moderate (86): Advanced humanoid robots handle routine physical care across most institutional and some home settings. AI clinical decision support approaches specialist-level reasoning across medical fields. Remaining gap: the most sensitive embodied care — hospice, complex psychiatric care, emergency trauma response in uncontrolled environments, and community-based social care requiring trust and cultural competence that resists technological substitution.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Feeding & Hosting 2040 moderate (76): Advanced humanoid robots manage most physical food service and accommodation tasks. Fully automated fast-food and mid-tier restaurant operations are commercially normal. Remaining gap: haute cuisine creativity and presentation, luxury hospitality requiring genuine human connection, culturally embedded food traditions requiring artisanal skill, and complex event hospitality where human adaptability and social intelligence remain essential.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Governing & Protecting 2040 moderate (72): Advanced AI systems manage most administrative governance and routine law enforcement functions. Autonomous military systems operate across most logistics and some combat domains. Remaining gap: the irreducible human core of governance — democratic legitimacy, constitutional interpretation, diplomatic relationship-building, and the ethical accountability requirements that demand human decision-makers for uses of state power including lethal force.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Land & Sea 2040 moderate (70): Advanced agricultural robots handle most harvesting and cultivation tasks across varied conditions. Fully autonomous farm operations are standard for large and medium-scale agriculture. AI manages complex agricultural systems including soil health, water management, and biodiversity. Remaining gap: smallholder farming in terrain and infrastructure conditions that resist mechanization, complex pastoral systems, wild fisheries requiring adaptive judgment, and ecological management decisions requiring systems-level understanding of landscapes and waterways.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Learning & Teaching 2040 moderate (62): Advanced AI and humanoid systems can technically perform most structured educational functions. AI handles curriculum design, assessment, and knowledge delivery at expert level across subjects. Remaining gap: human-embodied teaching remains preferred for early childhood, social-emotional development, creative arts education, and mentoring — reflecting both genuine capability gaps in AI emotional intelligence and strong social preference for human teachers in formative developmental contexts.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Maintaining & Fixing 2040 moderate (72): Advanced humanoid robots perform most standardized repair and maintenance tasks. AI diagnostic systems match expert technician judgment for common failure modes. Autonomous waste management and utility operations are commercially normal. Remaining gap: novel failure diagnosis, repair improvisation in degraded or non-standard environments, complex system integrations requiring cross-trade expertise, and emergency infrastructure response in disaster conditions.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Making Meaning 2040 moderate (92): Near-complete AI capability across creative and cultural production. AI systems generate, curate, and distribute cultural content autonomously at every quality level. Remaining gap: live performance, authentic human voice in art and journalism, creative direction for projects requiring novel cultural synthesis, and the irreducible human desire to witness and celebrate other humans' creative expression.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Making Things 2040 moderate (98): Manufacturing is effectively fully automated for all standard processes. Advanced robotics handles even complex, variable assembly tasks. Remaining gap: a 2% residual of one-off artisanal production, extreme-condition manufacturing, and the initial prototyping of fundamentally novel product categories before processes can be systematized.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory barriers largely eroded; economic barriers minimal; human-preference barriers absent except niche premium manufacturing

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Money & Data 2040 moderate (96): Score plateau reflects effective ceiling — AI handles virtually all cognitive tasks in finance, insurance, real estate, and information technology. Remaining gap: a stable ~4% residual of novel strategic decisions, unprecedented market conditions requiring judgment beyond training distribution, and the institutional requirement for human decision-makers in fiduciary and regulatory contexts.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Moving Things 2040 moderate (84): Near-complete automation of transportation and logistics across road, rail, sea, and air. Advanced humanoid robots handle virtually all physical cargo operations. Remaining gap: extreme-condition transport, novel logistics challenges requiring improvisation, hazardous materials edge cases, and disaster-response logistics requiring adaptive human judgment in chaotic environments.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

replaceability.primary_mechanism: Thinking & Leading 2040 moderate (95): Near-complete AI capability across professional, scientific, and administrative services. Remaining gap: a stable ~5% residual of executive leadership, organizational wisdom, novel scientific insight, and the interpersonal trust dimensions of professional relationships that clients and organizations continue to value in human advisors.
replaceability.barrier_erosion_note: Regulatory, economic, and human-preference barriers erode progressively with scenario intensity

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: AI excels at digitization workflows (OCR, handwriting recognition via Transkribus), metadata generation, image classification, and collection search (vector embeddings for semantic discovery). ML-powered conservation imaging (multispectral analysis) detects deterioration. LLMs can draft catalog entries and exhibition text. Remaining gap: provenance research requiring investigative judgment, acquisition decisions balancing scholarly, cultural, and financial considerations, physical handling and conservation of fragile objects, and the curatorial vision that shapes exhibitions into coherent narratives — an interpretive and aesthetic judgment AI cannot replicate. [v3.0 Cowork review: Archival work has 57% C_R (organizing records, classification, database management) — genuinely automatable. Current 19 is likely too low. Model 82 is too high because it misses the curatorial judgmen]
occupations.key_barrier_note: Collection interpretation, educational program design, and visitor engagement require human expertise in cultural and historical context.

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: Revenue management systems (IDeaS G3 RMS, Duetto, Atomize) use ML to set dynamic room rates across channels in real time, forecast demand by segment, and optimize group vs. transient mix. These systems outperform human intuition on pure pricing tasks. LLMs can draft competitive analysis reports and interpret market signals from event calendars. Remaining gap: strategic decisions during unprecedented disruptions (pandemics, natural disasters), negotiation of corporate/group contracts requiring relationship judgment, and cross-property portfolio strategy that requires understanding of brand positioning beyond historical data patterns.

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: AI assists with operational analytics (labor scheduling via HotSchedules/Fourth, energy management, predictive maintenance alerts, guest sentiment analysis from reviews). Chatbots handle routine guest requests. Remaining gap: the core of this role is physical presence leadership — walking the property, motivating staff, handling escalated complaints face-to-face, making real-time judgment calls during emergencies (fire, medical, security), maintaining community relationships, and embodying brand standards through personal interaction. Current AI/robotics cannot replicate the embodied leadership and crisis management this role demands. [v3.0 Cowork review: Hospitality role with 53% cognitive weight but the defining work is physical service delivery and human interaction. Decomposition overweights administrative/inventory tasks. Management component is p]

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: AI tools automate inventory forecasting (BlueCart, MarketMan), menu engineering analytics, labor scheduling, and food cost optimization. POS-integrated systems track waste and flag anomalies. Delivery platform algorithms manage order flow. Remaining gap: kitchen line coordination during service, real-time quality control (tasting, visual plating standards), staff training and motivation, handling health inspections, customer interaction during complaints, and the physical presence required to manage a fast-paced food service environment. Humanoid robotics not viable for the multi-tasking, heat-tolerant, spatially aware coordination this role requires.

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: AI-powered soil analysis platforms (CropX, Teralytic, Geonadir) combine satellite/drone multispectral imagery with IoT sensor data to map soil moisture, nutrient content, organic matter, and compaction across fields. ML models predict soil carbon sequestration potential and erosion risk. Automated lab analysis (spectroscopy-based) accelerates sample processing. Remaining gap: field sampling in variable terrain requiring physical soil core extraction, interpreting novel contamination or remediation scenarios, and the professional judgment needed for land-use recommendations that integrate ecological, agricultural, and regulatory considerations beyond pattern-matched historical data.

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: Autonomous floor-cleaning robots (Whiz by SoftBank, Nilfisk Liberty, Brain Corp-powered units) handle flat-floor vacuuming and scrubbing in commercial buildings. UV disinfection robots (Xenex, UVD Robots) sanitize rooms autonomously. Smart building systems automate HVAC, lighting, and basic fault detection. Remaining gap: restroom servicing requiring fine manipulation (restocking, unclogging, surface cleaning around fixtures), trash handling in varied receptacles, responding to spills and unexpected messes, minor repair tasks (changing lightbulbs, tightening fixtures), snow/ice removal, and navigating cluttered or multi-level environments. Current humanoid platforms lack the dexterity and reliability for the diverse physical tasks this role bundles. [v3.0 Cowork review: Repair/maintenance role with 67% cognitive weight but the defining work is hands-on physical repair in variable environments (Phi_U). Diagnostic component is increasingly AI-assisted but physical exec] [v3.0 Phase 9: maintaining_fixing recalibration. Tasks retagged for physical repair emphasis.]

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: Industrial laundry automation (Jensen Group, Kannegiesser, Girbau) handles washing, drying, ironing, and folding of standard flat linen (sheets, towels) at scale. Automated sorting systems use RFID tags. Remaining gap: hand-laundering of delicate or specialty garments (silk, beading, vintage fabrics), stain pre-treatment requiring visual inspection and material-specific judgment, pressing of structured garments (suits, pleated items) where human tactile feedback prevents damage, and the small-batch hotel valet service context where items vary constantly. Humanoid manipulation not refined enough for fabric handling at the required gentleness and precision. [v3.0 Cowork review: Hospitality role with 73% cognitive weight but the defining work is physical service delivery and human interaction. Decomposition overweights administrative/inventory tasks. Current 28 is more defens]

occupations.benchmark_anchor_2026: Smart vending machines (Cantaloupe, Vendron) with IoT connectivity report inventory levels, predict restocking schedules, and process cashless payments autonomously. Computer vision detects jammed products and alerts remotely. Some robotic restocking arms exist in prototype (e.g., Telexistence Model-T for convenience stores). Remaining gap: physical restocking of varied product shapes into machines at diverse locations, transporting stock from vehicles to machines, minor mechanical troubleshooting (coin jams, refrigeration issues), and route optimization across scattered locations requiring driving and site access. The role is constrained primarily by mobile manipulation in unstructured environments.

technology_events.impact_description: James Hargreaves invents the Spinning Jenny in Lancashire, England, allowing a single operator to work eight or more spools simultaneously, drastically reducing yarn production labor and initiating the spatial centralization of work into factories.

James Watt patents an improved steam engine with a separate condenser, reducing thermal waste and making steam power economically viable for widespread industrial application across manufacturing and transportation.
Steam-powered threshing machines begin replacing manual grain processing across agriculture. A single machine could process grain at rates previously requiring large teams of farm laborers.
Edmund Cartwright invents the power loom, mechanizing weaving and further displacing textile workers. This marked a major shift from hand-loom weavers to factory-based automated weaving.

Eli Whitney invents the cotton gin, mechanizing the separation of cotton fibers from seeds and exponentially increasing cotton processing throughput, paradoxically expanding demand for slave labor in harvesting.
Eli Whitney pioneers manufacturing with interchangeable parts for firearms, enabling mass production and reducing dependence on skilled craftspeople.

Joseph Marie Jacquard invents the programmable Jacquard loom, using punched cards to control weaving patterns automatically. This is considered an early form of programmable automation and a precursor to modern computing.

The Stockton-Darlington Railway becomes the first commercial railway to use steam locomotives for passenger and freight transport. This revolutionized land transportation and displaced horse-based logistics and workers.
Samuel Morse and Alfred Vail's telegraph becomes commercially viable, enabling instantaneous long-distance communication and transforming logistics, finance, and news dissemination.

Henry Bessemer patents a new steel-making process that dramatically increases production efficiency and reduces labor requirements. Cheap, abundant steel enabled mass production and construction throughout industry.

The Central Pacific and Union Pacific railroads meet at Promontory, Utah, completing the first transcontinental railroad in the United States. This unified national freight and passenger transport, displacing canal workers and overland freight handlers.

The Remington No. 1 typewriter becomes the first commercially successful typewriter, creating a new category of clerical worker while eventually displacing handwriting-based document work. Typewriters accelerated business communication and created demand for trained operators.

Alexander Graham Bell patents the telephone, enabling voice communication over distance. Telegraph operators were gradually displaced as telephone switchboard operators became the new intermediary role in communication networks.
Thomas Edison develops a practical electric light bulb, initiating the transition from gas lighting to electrical power and enabling extended work hours and factory night shifts.
The blackboard becomes standard classroom equipment by the 1840s, shifting pedagogical labor from purely oral tradition to standardized physical media for group instruction.
Lee De Forest invents the triode vacuum tube, enabling electronic amplification and switching, foundational for all subsequent electronic computing and telecommunications.

Henry Ford implements the moving assembly line for the Model T, reducing production time from 12 hours to 93 minutes per car. Assembly line production became the dominant manufacturing model and significantly reduced the skill requirements for factory workers.

KDKA in Pittsburgh conducts the first licensed commercial radio broadcast in the United States. Radio broadcasting created new media roles but displaced newspaper delivery routes and live entertainment venues.
Early television technologies are demonstrated, establishing the foundation for broadcast media that would mass-produce entertainment and information.
Alan Turing publishes 'On Computable Numbers' in November and December 1936, formalizing algorithms and computation, proving that a single universal machine could perform any computable task.
Chester Carlson invents xerography, enabling high-speed document copying and reducing reliance on manual document reproduction and messenger labor.

technology_events.event_name: Radar and Automated Fire Control Systems
technology_events.impact_description: Radar detection systems and automated fire control computers deployed at scale, replacing human computers who calculated artillery trajectories. First large-scale deployment of automated decision-making replacing human cognitive labor.

ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) becomes operational, the first general-purpose electronic computer capable of solving complex mathematical problems in seconds. It marked the beginning of the computing era and automated high-level calculation work.

The McDonald brothers revamp their operation with the Speedee Service System in 1948, eliminating waitstaff, reducing the menu to nine items, and redesigning the kitchen as a specialized assembly line.
The Standards Eastern Automatic Computer (SEAC) becomes one of the first fully electronic computers for scientific calculation and government use.

technology_events.event_name: American Airlines and IBM Collaborate on SABRE
technology_events.impact_description: American Airlines CEO C.R. Smith and IBM representative R. Blair Smith meet and begin collaboration on SABRE, an electronic reservation system to replace manual, handwritten card-based booking.

General Motors installs a Unimate robot on its assembly line to perform spot welding, marking the first use of an industrial robot in manufacturing. Robotic automation began replacing manual assembly and welding workers at scale.

Intel releases the 4004, the first general-purpose microprocessor, enabling computing power in small, affordable devices. Microprocessors enabled the personal computer revolution and eventually embedded automation in nearly every industrial process.

IBM releases the Personal Computer, making computing accessible to businesses and consumers. Personal computers displaced typewriter operators and enabled new types of knowledge work while making others more efficient.
Tim Berners-Lee invents the World Wide Web at CERN, creating the foundational infrastructure for global digital commerce, communication, and information sharing.

Amazon launches as an online bookseller, pioneering e-commerce at scale and automated warehouse operations. E-commerce displaced retail workers while creating demand for warehouse and logistics automation.
Google launches its search engine, automating information retrieval across the web. Google's search algorithms displaced research librarians and made information discovery nearly instantaneous.

technology_events.event_name: da Vinci Surgical System FDA Approval and Clinical Launch
technology_events.impact_description: The da Vinci Surgical Robot receives FDA approval, enabling minimally invasive robotic-assisted surgery. Robotic surgery systems changed surgical labor patterns and created new specialist roles.
technology_events.description: Robotic Surgery (da Vinci System FDA Approved) | da Vinci Surgical System FDA Approval

technology_events.impact_description: Amazon Mechanical Turk enables outsourcing of small tasks to distributed workers, automating task allocation and payment. MTurk pioneered digital gig work and automated the process of breaking tasks into microtasks.

Apple releases the iPhone, bringing computing power and connectivity to mobile devices and enabling location-based automation. The iPhone enabled new forms of logistics automation through GPS-based navigation and delivery tracking.
Amazon Web Services (AWS) becomes generally available, enabling cloud computing and automating infrastructure management previously requiring large IT teams.

Apple launches the App Store, enabling distributed software automation on mobile devices. The app economy created new roles in software development while automating previous manual tasks in productivity and communication.
The ImageNet breakthrough demonstrates that deep learning AI can achieve superhuman accuracy in computer vision tasks, fundamentally altering how machines interpret the physical world.
Breakthroughs in recurrent neural networks (LSTM) enable better sequence modeling and language understanding. RNN advances enabled AI to process time-series data and language more effectively.
Ian Goodfellow introduces Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), enabling AI to generate synthetic images and data. GANs became foundational for image generation and deepfake technology.

technology_events.event_name: AlphaGo Defeats World Champion Lee Sedol
technology_events.impact_description: DeepMind's AlphaGo defeats world Go champion Lee Sedol, demonstrating that deep reinforcement learning could master complex strategic games. This breakthrough showed AI could achieve intuitive reasoning in high-complexity domains.

Amazon Go opens a prototype cashier-less convenience store using computer vision and sensors to automate checkout. Cashier-less retail technology demonstrated potential for fully automated retail transactions.

OpenAI releases GPT-2, a large language model that can generate coherent text across many domains. GPT-2 demonstrated that transformer-based language models could perform diverse writing and reasoning tasks.

technology_events.event_name: Robot Density in China Reaches 140 per 10K Manufacturing Workers
technology_events.impact_description: China's manufacturing robot density reaches 140 robots per 10,000 workers, among the highest globally, reflecting massive automation of assembly and material handling. This accelerated displacement of manual manufacturing labor in the world's factory.

AlphaFold solves the fifty-year-old grand challenge of protein folding in 2020, proving that AI can dramatically accelerate fundamental scientific research by predicting 3D protein structures with unprecedented accuracy.

OpenAI releases GPT-3, a 175-billion parameter language model capable of performing many complex language tasks with minimal training examples. GPT-3 showed that large-scale language models could automate numerous knowledge-work tasks.

OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot begin development, creating AI systems to generate code from natural language descriptions. These tools demonstrated that AI could automate significant portions of software engineering work.
Consumer-facing ChatGPT is launched in late 2022, introducing conversational AI agents to the general public and shifting AI from research tool to widely accessible cognitive assistant.

Stable Diffusion is released as an open-source text-to-image generation model, democratizing AI image creation. The open-source availability enabled rapid adoption in creative and commercial applications.

Google launches Bard (later Gemini), its conversational AI competitor to ChatGPT, automating information access and reasoning tasks. Google's entry into conversational AI demonstrated the technology's mainstream adoption.

technology_events.event_name: OpenAI Adds Image Generation to ChatGPT
technology_events.impact_description: OpenAI adds image generation capabilities to ChatGPT, enabling multimodal AI in mainstream consumer products. Multimodal AI in consumer products affected graphic design and illustration jobs.

technology_events.event_name: Unitree G1 Humanoid Robot Announced for Commercial Sale
technology_events.impact_description: Unitree releases the G1 humanoid robot for commercial purchase at $13,500, making bipedal robot labor accessible beyond industrial applications. Affordable humanoid robots began entering warehouses, manufacturing, and service sectors.

Xiaomi launches an 81,000 square meter smartphone factory in Beijing operating 24/7 with zero human production workers. This fully automated facility became the largest completely dark manufacturing plant for consumer electronics globally.

technology_events.event_name: FDA AI Medical Device Approvals Surge
technology_events.impact_description: By mid-2025, the FDA has cleared roughly 873 AI algorithms for radiology, accounting for nearly 80% of all AI medical device authorizations, automating image interpretation and diagnostic triage.

METR (Model Evaluation and Threat Research) quantifies AI's 'effective time horizon,' showing that capabilities double every 7 months between 2019-2025, compressing to 88.6 days for models released since 2024.
Amazon lays off 16,000 workers in early 2026, with executive leadership explicitly linking workforce reductions to AI-driven efficiencies and strategic pivots.

technology_events.event_name: Humanoid Robots Enter Early Commercial Sale
technology_events.impact_description: By early 2026, humanoid robots cross the commercial availability threshold: Unitree ships 13,000-18,000 units globally in 2025 (G1 at $13,500, H1 at ~$90,000). Tesla begins pilot production of Optimus Gen 3 at Fremont (hands upgraded to 22 DOF/50 actuators), but robots remain in R&D performing learning and data collection, not productive tasks (per Q4 2025 earnings call, Jan 28 2026). First external customer deliveries targeted late 2026; consumer sales targeted end of 2027.

macro_industry.note: From 1900, macro_industry splits into Making Things, Building Things, Moving Things. These labels are for the pre-split period only.
macro_industry.source_basis: Bairoch (1988), Mitchell (2003), Cambridge Group occupational reconstructions

occupations.description: Spinners, weavers, dyers, bleachers, finishers. The dominant industrial occupation through 1870.
occupations.share_trend: Declining from ~30% in 1800 to ~20% by 1870 as industry diversifies

occupations.description: Masons, bricklayers, plasterers, roofers, road builders. Grows with urbanization.
Coal, iron, copper, tin, quarrying. Grows sharply with steam power demand.
Blacksmiths, foundry workers, toolmakers, nailmakers. The skilled core of early industry.
Millers, bakers, brewers, butchers, sugar refiners.
Carpenters, coopers, wheelwrights, furniture makers, shipwrights.
occupations.share_trend: Declining as metal replaces wood in manufacturing

occupations.description: Tanners, shoemakers, saddlers, glovers. One of the oldest crafts.
Dockworkers, porters, warehouse laborers. Grows with trade volume.

macro_services.note: Services disaggregates gradually: Governing & Protecting splits off at 1900, Buying & Selling at 1950, Learning & Teaching and Care & Health at 1960, Money & Data at 1970. These labels cover the residual blob.

occupations.description: Housemaids, cooks, valets, governesses, laundresses. The single largest service occupation until ~1920.
occupations.share_trend: Declining from ~40% in 1800 to ~25% by 1900, ~10% by 1940

occupations.description: Military personnel, constables, watchmen. Fluctuates with wars.
occupations.share_trend: Volatile — spikes during wartime, otherwise gradually rising with state capacity

occupations.description: Shopkeepers, market traders, hawkers, commercial agents, brokers.
occupations.share_trend: Rising steadily with urbanization and consumer economy

occupations.description: Publicans, innkeepers, waiters, hotel staff, cooks (commercial).
Tax collectors, customs officers, postal workers, clerks, magistrates.
Priests, ministers, monks, nuns, missionaries.
Schoolmasters, governesses, university lecturers. Grows with public education.
occupations.share_trend: Rising sharply after 1850 with compulsory education laws

occupations.description: Physicians, surgeons, apothecaries, midwives, dentists.

land_sea.description: Agricultural workforce — applies across all periods
land_sea.note: Land & Sea is a real territory from 1800 onward. These historical labels supplement the modern ISCO occupations for the pre-1900 period when clicking the territory shape.
land_sea.source_basis: Bairoch (1988), FAO historical data, Cambridge Group

occupations.description: Agricultural workers, harvest hands, field laborers. The majority of all human employment until ~1900.
occupations.share_trend: Declining as mechanization begins, but still dominant globally

occupations.description: Tenant farmers, peasant farmers, crofters. Own or lease small plots.
occupations.share_trend: Declining in Europe with enclosure and consolidation; stable elsewhere

occupations.description: Shepherds, herdsmen, cattle drovers, dairymaids.
Sea fishers, river fishers, whalers, oyster gatherers.
Lumberjacks, charcoal burners, bark strippers, forest wardens.
Land stewards, bailiffs, overseers, plantation managers.
Livestock droving, cattle herding, sheep mustering, and animal husbandry labor across pastoral and agricultural regions.
occupations.share_trend: Declining with rail transport and fencing; regionally persistent through 1870

occupations.description: Vegetable growers, fruit growers, nurserymen supplying urban markets.

land_sea.description: Agriculture, fishing, forestry, and mining — the work that feeds and fuels.
land_sea.panel_note: From four in five workers in 1800 to roughly one in four today. Still the world's largest employer.

making_things.description: Manufacturing — from textiles to semiconductors, everything that gets made in a factory.

building_things.description: Construction, architecture, and civil engineering — the people who build and design the built environment.

moving_things.description: Transport, logistics, and warehousing — moving goods and people from one place to another.

buying_selling.description: Retail, wholesale, and personal services — the work of selling things and serving customers face to face.

money_data.description: Finance, insurance, information technology, and data — where money moves and information is processed.

care_health.description: Healthcare, social work, and caregiving — the work of keeping people alive and well.

learning_teaching.description: Education at every level — from preschool to university, and everything taught in between.

making_meaning.description: Arts, media, entertainment, and sport — the work that creates culture and tells stories.

governing_protecting.description: Government, law, defense, and public safety — the work of running and protecting societies.

feeding_hosting.description: Restaurants, hotels, food service, and tourism — the work of feeding and hosting people.

maintaining_fixing.description: Utilities, repair, and essential services — the work that keeps everything else running.

thinking_leading.description: Management, consulting, science, and professional strategy — the work of deciding what to do.

macro_industry.description: The industrial workforce before it splits into Making Things, Building Things, and Moving Things. Textiles, mining, metalwork, construction, and early manufacturing.
macro_industry.panel_note: From 1900, this territory disaggregates as the data allows finer distinctions between types of industrial work.

macro_services.description: Every non-agricultural, non-industrial job — from domestic servants and soldiers to merchants, teachers, and doctors.
macro_services.panel_note: Services disaggregates gradually: Governing & Protecting splits off at 1900, Buying & Selling at 1950, Learning & Teaching and Care & Health at 1960, Money & Data at 1970.
